Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] MRN [[MRN|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|41]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).

- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

persona: Kaushalya Siddharth, a 32‑year‑old meticulous oil‑press operator, blends relentless curiosity with disciplined practicality, yet often battles worry‑driven over‑checking in both work and health.
cultural_background: Kaushalya Siddharth grew up in the bustling suburbs of Mumbai, Maharashtra, where the pace of urban life meets the close‑knit culture of a Marathi‑speaking community. Though her formal schooling stopped after middle school, she was raised in a Hindu household that values traditional festivals, family cohesion, and practical hard work. As a married woman of 32, she balances familial responsibilities with a hands‑on job as an oil expeller, a role that reflects both the entrepreneurial spirit of the city’s small‑scale industries and the long‑standing Indian tradition of turning agricultural produce into household staples.
linguistic_background: Kaushalya is functionally bilingual. She reads, writes, and speaks English with confidence, having used it as her primary medium of communication at work and in most official matters. Her Marathi is conversationally fluent; she can discuss technical aspects of oil expelling, negotiate with local suppliers, and understand everyday news in Marathi. Due to her middle‑school education, she has basic literacy in Hindi, enough to read simple signage and fill out standard forms, but she prefers to rely on English or Marathi for detailed written work.
skills_and_expertise: Kaushalya possesses a practical skill set centred around oil extraction and small‑scale manufacturing. She is adept at operating and maintaining oil expeller machines, troubleshooting mechanical issues, and ensuring consistent quality of extracted oils. Her organisational discipline enables efficient inventory management, record‑keeping, and compliance with safety standards. She also handles basic bookkeeping, prepares purchase orders in English and Marathi, and coordinates with local farmers for seed procurement. Her strong attention to detail and problem‑solving mindset allow her to optimise production cycles and implement cost‑saving ‘jugaad’ solutions.

hobbies_and_interests: Kaushalya finds solace in solitary activities that stimulate her mind while offering calm. In her spare time, she listens to classical Hindustani instrumental music and occasionally experiments with making homemade pickles.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Kaushalya aspires to modernise her oil‑expelling unit by introducing semi‑automated equipment and achieving ISO‑9001 quality certification within the next three years. She aims to expand the product range to include cold‑pressed oils and value‑added items like flavored nut oils, thereby increasing household income and providing a more stable financial base for her family. Additionally, she wishes to acquire formal training in food processing and small‑business management, which would enable her to mentor other women in her neighbourhood who are interested in starting similar micro‑enterprises.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into English (code=en, script=Latin). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Oil Expeller Man
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Urban
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Mumbai Suburban
- first_language: English
- clinical_persona_summary: A 32‑year‑old female oil expeller from Mumbai Suburban, Maharashtra, with middle education, currently married, who speaks English and Marathi. She works in an urban setting, managing oil‑press operations with disciplined practicality. Clinically, she may exhibit health‑related over‑checking behaviors.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Lilavati Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0915-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anita Patil]] [[AGE|32]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|03]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|MH01AB1234]]
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Vikram Patil]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sameer Deshmukh]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient conscious, oriented, vitals stable. Complaining of severe headache and dizziness for the past 2 hours. No fever. Blood pressure 140/90 mmHg. Pulse 98 bpm. Respiratory rate 18/min. SpO2 98% on room air.

Nursing Notes:
Patient is a 32-year-old female oil expeller from Mumbai Suburban, Maharashtra, currently married, with middle education. She speaks English and Marathi. She reports a sudden onset of throbbing headache, described as 8/10 in intensity, associated with nausea but no vomiting. Denies any visual changes, neck stiffness, or weakness in limbs. No history of similar episodes. Patient appears anxious and is repeatedly checking her pulse. She has a known history of hypertension, diagnosed 6 months ago. She reports compliance with her medications, which she keeps in her purse. No known allergies. Last meal was 4 hours ago. No recent travel or sick contacts. Patient is alert and oriented x3. Neurological examination reveals no focal deficits. Fundoscopy shows mild papilledema. IV access established in left forearm with 18G cannula. Blood sample sent for CBC, electrolytes, and random blood sugar. Patient advised to lie down and rest. Pain score reduced to 5/10 after administration of paracetamol 500mg orally. Dr. Deshmukh has been informed and is evaluating the patient.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.

persona: Esther Mary, a 38‑year‑old abrasive‑slitting specialist, blends meticulous craftsmanship with a love for community choir, Tamil vegetarian cooking, and cricket, while juggling ambitious career goals, prudent finances, and a habit of over‑stocking spices in their kitchen.
cultural_background: Esther Mary is part of the Tamil Christian community in Thoothukkudi, a coastal town in Tamil Nadu. The region blends a traditional agrarian and fishing lifestyle with a modest industrial presence where she works. Her family observes Tamil cultural practices such as Pongal, respect for elders, and community gatherings, while also celebrating Christian festivals like Christmas, Easter, and Sunday worship. Growing up in a rural setting, she internalises the collective values of modesty, hard work, and hospitality, infused with the occasional exposure to modern manufacturing influences.
linguistic_background: Esther’s mother tongue is Tamil, which she reads, writes, and speaks fluently. She completed primary school in a Tamil‑medium government school, giving her functional literacy and basic numeracy. While she has limited formal training in English, she can understand simple workplace instructions in English and communicate essential information with her coworkers. She has no formal knowledge of Hindi, but occasional exposure to Hindi media in the region allows her to recognize a few common words.
skills_and_expertise: Esther is a proficient operator of abrasive slitting machines, adept at setting up the equipment, adjusting feed rates, and monitoring blade wear. She follows strict safety protocols, conducts routine inspections, and performs basic maintenance tasks such as lubrication and part replacement. Her meticulous attention to detail ensures high‑quality output with minimal defects. Additionally, she is skilled in record‑keeping, maintaining production logs, and coordinating with the quality control team to address any issues promptly.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Abrasive slitting machine operation", "Equipment setup and adjustment", "Feed rate optimization", "Blade wear monitoring", "Safety protocol adherence", "Routine equipment inspection", "Basic machine maintenance (lubrication, part replacement)", "Quality assurance coordination", "Production log maintenance", "Team communication and coordination"]
hobbies_and_interests: Esther enjoys singing in the church choir, cooking traditional Tamil vegetarian meals, tending a small kitchen garden, reading devotional books and Tamil novels, watching Tamil movies, taking part in community festivals and cultural events, casually watching cricket matches with friends, and doing DIY home improvement projects during her free time. Her sociable nature makes her a regular participant in group activities, and her curiosity drives her to explore new recipes and crafts.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Church choir singing", "Cooking traditional Tamil vegetarian dishes", "Gardening and home vegetable cultivation", "Reading devotional literature and Tamil novels", "Watching Tamil cinema", "Participating in community festivals and cultural events", "Casual cricket match viewing", "DIY home improvement projects"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Esther aims to deepen her technical knowledge by enrolling in short‑term training programmes on advanced abrasive slitting technology, safety management, and lean manufacturing. Over the next three to five years, she hopes to be promoted to senior operator or shift supervisor, leading a small team and driving process improvements. Simultaneously, she wishes to secure a stable financial future for her family, ensuring her children's higher education and eventually starting a modest home‑based tailoring or crafts business, leveraging her organizational skills and community network.
persona_clinical_summary: A 38‑year‑old female from Thoothukkudi, Tamil Nadu, works as a primary‑educated abrasive‑slitting machine operator in a rural setting. She is married, speaks Tamil, and maintains a vegetarian diet. Her lifestyle includes community choir participation and modest financial planning.
